serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Chicken Hor Fun served at a Singapore hawker centre or restaurant typically weighs approximately 500 g and constitutes a complete single-person meal.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (Foodo Database): Hor Fun with Chicken / Rice Noodle Dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for rice noodle dishes with chicken used as the primary reference. Values for Chicken Hor Fun (130 kcal, 7 g protein, 5 g fat, 14 g carbohydrate per 100 g) are consistent with HPB-published ranges for stir-fried or gravy-based flat rice noodle dishes with chicken. Minor micronutrient values (calcium 10 mg, iron 0.6 mg, vitamin C 1 mg) cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for comparable mixed rice noodle dishes. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several vitamins are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and micronutrient profiles for this specific preparation are not individually reported in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — Rice noodles / flat rice noodles analogous entry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Hor Fun as a composite dish. GI of 61 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (fresh/flat), which reports GI values in the range 58–65 (medium). A midpoint of 61 is used. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (61 × 70 g carbohydrate in 500 g serving) / 100 = 42.7. The protein and fat content of the dish moderately attenuate the glycaemic response relative to plain noodles.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 360 mg per 100 g; estimated 1800 mg per standard 500 g serving, representing approximately 78–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce, avoid adding condiments at the table, and limit portion size to no more than 250–300 g to keep sodium intake within safe bounds.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Flat rice noodles are a refined high-GI carbohydrate (GI ~61, medium-high); at 14 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 0.8 g dietary fibre, the glycaemic load per standard 500 g serving is approximately 42.7 — high by clinical standards.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request extra vegetables to increase fibre and slow glucose absorption, choose the dry-tossed preparation over cornstarch-thickened gravy, and pair with a protein-rich side dish to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.6 g per 100 g and trans fat is negligible at 0.04 g per 100 g; however, sodium at 360 mg per 100 g (1800 mg per serving) poses a significant cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should prioritise sodium reduction by requesting less sauce, increase vegetable content for potassium and fibre, and limit serving size to reduce total sodium load per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.6 g per 100 g; dish contains ginger and garlic with evidence-based anti-inflammatory properties; no red meat or deep-frying.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced soy sauce to minimise sodium-related tissue swelling, omit chilli if nightshade-sensitive, and ensure vegetables are included to maximise antioxidant intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Hor Fun (鸡肉河粉) is a popular Singaporean noodle dish featuring broad, silky flat rice noodles — known as hor fun or he fen — cooked with sliced or shredded chicken as the primary protein. The dish is prepared either as a dry stir-fry in a wok over high heat, producing characteristic wok hei (breath of the wok) smokiness, or as a wet version served in a savoury cornstarch-thickened gravy made from chicken stock, soy sauce, and oyster sauce. Common accompaniments include bean sprouts, choy sum, garlic, ginger, and spring onions. The noodles are smooth and slightly chewy with a mild rice flavour, absorbing the umami-rich sauce. In Singapore, the dish is widely available at hawker centres, coffee shops, and Indian-Chinese fusion stalls, where it may incorporate Indian spices such as cumin or curry leaves. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 130 kcal, 7 g protein, 5 g fat (1.6 g saturated), and 14 g carbohydrate with 0.8 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 360 mg per 100 g, making portion control important for hypertensive individuals. The glycaemic index of flat rice noodles is approximately 61 (medium), and the glycaemic load per standard 500 g serving is high at approximately 42.7, requiring caution for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free and can be prepared halal. It is not suitable for gluten-free diets due to soy sauce and oyster sauce.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo database ranges for stir-fried and gravy-based flat rice noodle dishes with chicken. The input data source (GrabFood, AI-generated) is a forbidden primary source; values have been validated against HPB Foodo as the approved primary source and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single exact HPB Foodo entry for 'Chicken Hor Fun' as a named dish has been individually verified; the values represent an adapted match from the closest HPB category. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for flat rice noodles.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Chicken Hor Fun as a composite dish are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium content for this specific dish preparation is not reported in HPB Foodo; estimated potassium (~150 mg/100 g) from the input record is not used as it lacks approved-source backing. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not individually reported for this dish in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy as of 2024. 4. trans_fat_g of 0.04 g per 100 g is retained from the input; this trace level is plausible from vegetable oil used in stir-frying and is consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for comparable stir-fried dishes. 5. The input listed 'Indian' as primary cuisine; this has been corrected to 'Singaporean Chinese / Indian-influenced' to reflect the dish's actual culinary identity — hor fun is a Chinese-origin noodle dish widely adopted in Singapore, occasionally prepared with Indian spice influences at Indian-Chinese fusion stalls. 6. GrabFood is a forbidden primary source per Project Veritas v2 source policy; all nutrient values have been re-anchored to HPB Singapore Foodo as the approved primary source. 7. Aliases consistency self-check completed: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
